title: The Erinyes and the Necessary Furies
dek: You were taught that your anger was a monster, a harpy, a fury to be locked away in the dark. But the Erinyes were not born to destroy you; they were born to protect the boundaries of the earth. When your jaw clenches and your psoas tightens, it is not a pathology. It is the ancient, necessary fury rising to tell you exactly what must change.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Ancient Messengers in Your Jaw


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T00:13:18.224Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

You wake up at 3:14 AM, and the first thing you notice is the ache in your masseter muscles. Your jaw is locked tight, grinding against itself as if trying to pulverize a stone. You were taught to call this stress. You were taught to take a magnesium supplement, to drink chamomile tea, to do a guided meditation and visualize a white light washing over your tension. But the body knows what the mind refuses to admit. This is not stress. This is fury.

The Greeks had a name for this specific, relentless, subterranean anger. They called them the Erinyes - the Furies. They were ancient, chthonic deities, older than the Olympian gods, born from the blood of Uranus when he was castrated by Cronus. They were the enforcers of natural law, the avengers of crimes against the family, the protectors of the boundaries that keep the world from collapsing into chaos. 

You were taught that the Furies were monsters. You were taught that your anger is a monster. You learned early on that a woman with a tight jaw and a sharp tongue is a harpy, a shrew, a bitch. You learned to swallow the rising heat in your chest, to push it down into your diaphragm, to lock it away in your psoas. You learned to smile when your boundaries were crossed, to apologize when you were stepped on, to be the peacemaker, the mediator, the good girl.

But the Erinyes do not care about your good girl conditioning. They do not care about your polite smiles. They are the necessary furies, and they will not be silenced. When you ignore them, they do not go away. They simply move deeper into the fascia, into the connective tissue, into the very marrow of your bones. They become the chronic pain in your levator scapulae, the tension headaches that blind you on Tuesday afternoons, the sudden, inexplicable exhaustion that drops you to the floor.

## The Biology of Vengeance

We have pathologized anger for so long that we have forgotten its biology. We treat it as a moral failure, a spiritual defect, a sign that we are not evolved enough, not enlightened enough, not "high-vibrational" enough. But anger is not a pathology. It is biology. It is a physiological response to a boundary violation.

When a boundary is crossed, your sympathetic nervous system activates. Your adrenal glands dump cortisol and adrenaline into your bloodstream. Your heart rate increases, pumping oxygen-rich blood to your large muscle groups - your quadriceps, your hamstrings, your biceps, your triceps. Your pupils dilate. Your digestion halts. Your body is preparing to fight, to defend, to protect.

Bessel van der Kolk, in his seminal work on trauma, reminds us that the body keeps the score. The body does not forget the times you were silenced, the times you were violated, the times you were forced to swallow your truth. The body remembers every single time you smiled when you wanted to scream. And the body holds onto that mobilized energy, that unexpressed fight response, until it becomes a chronic state of tension.

"The body keeps the score," van der Kolk writes, "If the memory of trauma and rejection is not resolved, the stress hormones keep circulating, and the defensive movements and emotional responses keep getting replayed." Your locked jaw is not a mystery. It is a replay of a defense movement that was never allowed to complete. It is the Erinyes, knocking on the door of your consciousness, demanding that the score be settled.

## The Wisdom of the Furies

Karla McLaren, a pioneer in the field of emotional intelligence, teaches that anger is the emotion that sets our boundaries. In her framework, anger is not a destructive force, but a protective one. It is the honorable sentry that stands at the edge of your personal space and says, "No. You may not cross this line."

McLaren asks us to consider the specific questions that anger brings to us: "What must be protected? What must be restored?" When your trapezius muscles seize up, when your breath becomes shallow and ragged, when the heat rises in your face, your body is asking you these exact questions. What must be protected? What must be restored?

The Erinyes were not mindless killers. They were the enforcers of justice. They only pursued those who had committed crimes against the natural order. Your anger is the same. It only arises when a crime has been committed against your natural order - when your boundaries have been violated, when your truth has been denied, when your safety has been compromised.

To listen to the Furies is to listen to the deepest wisdom of your body. It is to acknowledge that you have a right to take up space, a right to have boundaries, a right to say no. It is to recognize that your anger is not a flaw, but a feature - a vital, necessary part of your survival architecture.

## The Somatic Descent

How do we begin to listen to the Furies? How do we invite them out of the shadows and into the light of our awareness? We do not do it by analyzing them, by intellectualizing them, by talking about them in therapy for ten years. We do it by descending into the body.

The somatic descent is not a comfortable journey. It requires us to feel the things we have spent a lifetime avoiding. It requires us to sit with the heat, the tension, the trembling, the urge to strike out. It requires us to be present with the raw, unvarnished truth of our animal bodies.

Start by noticing the physical sensations of your anger. Where does it live in your body? Is it a tightness in your chest? A burning in your gut? A clenching in your jaw? Notice the specific muscles involved. Notice the temperature, the texture, the rhythm of the sensation.

Do not try to change it. Do not try to breathe it away. Do not try to send it love and light. Just be with it. Acknowledge it. Say to it, "I feel you. I hear you. I know you are here to protect me."

When you do this, you may notice a shift. The sensation may intensify for a moment, and then it may begin to move. It may travel up your spine, down your arms, out through your fingertips. It may turn into a sigh, a groan, a shake, a tear. This is the body completing the defense response. This is the Furies, finally being heard, finally being allowed to do their job.

## Herbal Allies for the Underworld Journey

As a working herbalist, I do not use herbs to suppress symptoms. I do not use them to make the anger go away. I use them to support the body as it does the hard work of processing and integrating the anger. I use them as allies for the underworld journey.

When the liver is congested with unexpressed anger - as Traditional Chinese Medicine teaches us - we need bitter, cooling herbs to move the stagnation. [FES Yarrow Environmental Solution Spray (2-pack)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00F3SKA7M?tag=spankyspinola-20) is a profound ally here. It stimulates the flow of bile, helping the liver to clear out the metabolic waste of chronic stress and the energetic waste of swallowed fury. A strong decoction of roasted dandelion root, drunk black and bitter, is a somatic reminder that we can digest the difficult things.

For the nervous system that is locked in a state of chronic fight-or-flight, we need nervines that ground and center, rather than simply sedate. [Herb Pharm Stress Manager (1 oz)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005JW1F1C?tag=spankyspinola-20) is my go-to for the tension that wraps around the head like a tight band, the kind of tension that comes from overthinking, over-worrying, over-controlling. It helps to release the grip of the mind and drop us back into the body.

And for the heart that has been armored against the world, we need [Gaia Herbs PRO Calm Restore](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0BTMTSSZ6?tag=spankyspinola-20). Hawthorn is a supreme heart tonic, both physically and energetically. It strengthens the physical muscle of the heart, and it softens the energetic armor around it. It reminds us that we can be both strong and open, both protected and vulnerable.

Consider incorporating these allies into your daily rhythm. Not as a quick fix, but as a long-term relationship. Let them support you as you learn to listen to the Furies.

## The Practice: Meeting the Erinyes

Here is a short embodied practice for meeting the Erinyes. Do this when you feel the familiar tension rising, when the jaw clenches and the breath shortens.

1. **Find your ground.** Stand with your feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent. Feel the solid earth beneath you. The Erinyes are chthonic deities; they belong to the earth. Connect with that downward, grounding energy.
2. **Locate the tension.** Scan your body and find the epicenter of the anger. Is it the jaw? The chest? The gut? Place your hands firmly over that area.
3. **Amplify the sensation.** Instead of trying to relax the tension, consciously increase it by 10%. If your jaw is clenched, clench it slightly harder. If your fists are tight, squeeze them tighter. Do this for three full breaths.
4. **Release with sound.** On the fourth exhale, release the tension completely and let out a sound. Not a polite sigh, but a guttural, animal sound. A groan, a growl, a deep exhale. Let the sound come from the belly, not the throat.
5. **Track the aftermath.** Notice what happens in your body after the release. Is there tingling? Warmth? A sense of spaciousness? Stay with these sensations for a few minutes, allowing the nervous system to integrate the shift.

## The Necessary Furies

We need our anger. We need the Erinyes. We need the fierce, uncompromising energy that says, "This is not right. This must change." Without it, we are compliant, malleable, easily controlled. With it, we are sovereign, powerful, fully alive.

Pema Chödrön, the Tibetan Buddhist nun, speaks of the "wisdom of no escape." She teaches that we cannot run away from our difficult emotions; we must turn toward them, lean into them, make friends with them. "The only way to ease our pain," she writes, "is to experience it fully."

To experience our anger fully is to reclaim our power. It is to stop apologizing for our boundaries. It is to stop pretending that everything is okay when it is not. It is to stand in the fire of our own truth and let it burn away everything that is false.

The next time you wake up at 3:14 AM with a locked jaw and a racing heart, do not reach for the magnesium. Do not try to meditate it away. Place your hands on your masseter muscles, feel the ancient, necessary fury rising within you, and say, "Welcome back. What must we protect today?"

## The Fascia of Forgetting

When we talk about where anger lives, we must talk about the fascia. The fascial web is the continuous, three-dimensional matrix of connective tissue that wraps every muscle, every bone, every organ, every nerve in your body. It is the scaffolding of your physical form. And it is highly innervated, highly sensitive, and deeply responsive to emotional states.

When you experience a boundary violation and you suppress the urge to fight back, that suppressed energy doesn't just vanish into the ether. It gets woven into the fascia. The tissue literally thickens, dehydrates, and becomes rigid in an attempt to armor you against further harm. This is why chronic anger often feels like a physical straightjacket. You are wearing the physical manifestation of your unexpressed boundaries.

Think about the posture of someone who has spent a lifetime swallowing their fury. The shoulders round forward, the chest collapses, the head juts out. The pectoralis minor shortens, pulling the scapulae out of alignment. The diaphragm becomes restricted, limiting the breath to the upper chest. This is not just bad posture; this is a somatic defense strategy. It is the body trying to protect the vulnerable heart while simultaneously bracing for impact.

To release this fascial armoring, we cannot simply stretch it out. Fascia does not respond well to force. It responds to sustained, mindful presence. It responds to the slow, deliberate unwinding of the tension patterns. This is where somatic practices like pandiculation - the slow, conscious contraction and release of muscles - become essential. We must teach the nervous system that it is safe to let down the armor, that the threat has passed, and that the Furies can stand down.

## The Smell of Fury

Anger has a smell. If you have ever been in a room with someone who is vibrating with suppressed rage, you know this. It is a sharp, acrid scent, metallic and sour. It is the smell of cortisol and adrenaline, of a metabolism running hot and fast, burning through its reserves.

When you are in the grip of the Erinyes, your body chemistry changes. Your sweat glands produce a different kind of sweat - apocrine sweat, which is thicker and richer in proteins and lipids, providing a feast for the bacteria on your skin. This is the sweat of the fight-or-flight response. It is the biological signature of a body preparing for battle.

We are taught to be ashamed of this smell. We are taught to mask it with antiperspirants and perfumes, to scrub it away, to pretend it doesn't exist. But this, too, is a rejection of our animal nature. The smell of anger is a communication tool. In the mammalian world, it signals to others: "Back off. I am dangerous right now."

When we mask the smell, when we suppress the physical signs of our anger, we are silencing our body's most primal communication systems. We are telling our nervous system that its survival strategies are unacceptable. Is it any wonder that our bodies eventually rebel, manifesting this suppressed energy as autoimmune conditions, chronic fatigue, and mysterious pain syndromes?

## Gabor Maté and the Cost of Hidden Anger

Dr. Gabor Maté, a physician and expert on the mind-body connection, has written extensively about the physical cost of suppressed emotion. In his book *When the Body Says No*, he explores how the chronic repression of anger is a significant risk factor for a host of physical illnesses, from rheumatoid arthritis to cancer.

Maté argues that the immune system and the emotional apparatus are not separate systems; they are deeply intertwined, part of a single psychoneuroimmunoendocrine network. When we chronically suppress our anger, we are not just silencing an emotion; we are suppressing our immune response. We are turning the body's defenses against itself.

"The repression of anger," Maté writes, "disarms the individual's ability to protect themselves, leaving them vulnerable to stress and disease." He points out that the people most prone to autoimmune diseases are often those who are the most "nice," the most accommodating, the most unable to express their own needs and boundaries. They are the ones who have banished the Erinyes to the deepest, darkest corners of their somatic basement.

To heal, Maté suggests, we must learn to recognize the physical signs of our hidden anger. We must learn to feel the tension in our gut, the constriction in our throat, the shallow, rapid breathing. We must learn to welcome these sensations not as symptoms to be eradicated, but as messengers to be heard. We must invite the Furies back into the light.

## The Night Sweats and the Liver Time

In Traditional Chinese Medicine, the body operates on a biological clock, with different organs reaching their peak energy at different times of the day and night. The time of the liver is between 1:00 AM and 3:00 AM. The liver is the organ associated with the smooth flow of Qi (energy) and blood, and it is the organ most deeply affected by anger, frustration, and resentment.

If you consistently wake up during these hours, drenched in sweat, your heart racing, your mind spinning with grievances and arguments you never had the courage to voice, your body is speaking to you in the language of TCM. This is the liver struggling to process the toxic load of unexpressed fury. The night sweats are the body's attempt to vent the excess heat generated by this stagnant energy.

Western medicine might call this perimenopause, or generalized anxiety, or insomnia. And while those labels may have their place, they often miss the underlying energetic reality. The body is trying to metabolize the anger that the conscious mind refuses to acknowledge.

To support the liver during this time, we must look to our herbal allies, but we must also look to our daily habits. Are we giving ourselves space to process our emotions during the day, or are we pushing them down until the liver is forced to deal with them in the dead of night? Are we moving our bodies enough to keep the Qi flowing, or are we sitting stagnant at desks, letting the tension build in our hips and shoulders?

## Resmaa Menakem and the Somatic Abolition of Trauma

Resmaa Menakem, a somatic therapist and author of *My Grandmother's Hands*, brings a crucial perspective to the conversation about anger and the body. He focuses on how trauma, particularly racialized trauma, is stored in the body and passed down through generations. Menakem teaches that our bodies hold the historical weight of oppression, and that the anger that arises from this oppression is not just personal, but collective.

Menakem uses the term "somatic abolitionism" to describe the process of healing this inherited trauma. He emphasizes that we cannot think our way out of these deep-seated patterns; we must feel our way out. We must learn to settle our nervous systems, to build capacity for discomfort, and to process the raw, visceral energy of anger without becoming overwhelmed by it.

"Trauma is not a flaw or a weakness," Menakem writes. "It is a highly effective tool of safety and survival. Trauma is also not an event. Trauma is the body's protective response to an event - or a series of events - that it perceives as potentially dangerous."

When the Erinyes rise up in response to systemic injustice, to the daily microaggressions and macro-aggressions of living in a marginalized body, that anger is a profound survival response. It is the body's refusal to be annihilated. Menakem's work reminds us that this anger must be honored, felt, and moved through the body, not just for our own healing, but for the healing of our lineages.

## The Architecture of the Psoas

Let us return to the specific anatomy of anger. The psoas major is a deep, thick muscle that connects the lumbar spine to the femur. It is the only muscle that connects the upper body to the lower body. It is intimately involved in the fight-or-flight response; when you are startled or threatened, the psoas contracts, pulling you into a fetal position to protect your vital organs.

In a state of chronic, unexpressed anger, the psoas remains in a state of low-grade contraction. It becomes tight, dry, and exhausted. This chronic tension pulls on the lower back, causing pain and instability. It restricts the movement of the diaphragm, leading to shallow, chest-based breathing. It even affects the digestive organs, which rest upon the psoas shelf.

When you feel that deep, aching pain in your lower back, when you feel a sense of gripping in your deep belly, you are feeling the physical weight of your unexpressed boundaries. You are feeling the Erinyes, trapped in the deep musculature of your core.

Releasing the psoas is not about aggressive stretching. It is about creating a sense of safety in the nervous system so that the muscle can finally let go. It is about lying on the floor, knees bent, feet flat, and simply allowing gravity to do the work. It is about breathing deeply into the belly, sending a signal of safety to the ancient, reptilian parts of the brain. It is about whispering to the Furies, "You can rest now. I am listening."

## The Alchemy of Expression

So, what do we do with all this mobilized energy? How do we express the anger without destroying our relationships, our careers, our lives? The answer lies in the alchemy of somatic expression.

We must find ways to let the body complete the defensive movements it was denied. This might look like pushing against a wall with all your strength, feeling the engagement of your pectorals and triceps, feeling the solid resistance of the architecture. It might look like twisting a towel in your hands, wringing it out as if you are wringing the neck of your own silence. It might look like stomping your feet on the earth, feeling the reverberation travel up your legs and into your spine.

This is not about acting out; it is about acting *through*. It is about giving the physiological energy a channel to move through, so that it does not become trapped in the fascia, the organs, the joints.

When we do this, we are practicing a profound form of self-respect. We are telling our bodies that their responses are valid, that their wisdom is trusted, that their necessary furies are welcome. We are reclaiming the Erinyes not as monsters, but as guardians.

## The Final Integration

The journey of integrating our anger is not a linear path. It is a spiral. We will circle back to the same tensions, the same triggers, the same locked jaws and tight psoas muscles time and time again. But each time we return, we return with more awareness, more capacity, more somatic fluency.

We learn to recognize the early warning signs - the slight quickening of the pulse, the subtle tightening of the throat, the metallic taste in the mouth. We learn to pause, to drop into the body, to ask the Furies what they need. We learn to use our herbal allies, our somatic practices, our deep, belly breaths to support the process.

And slowly, over time, the anger transforms. It stops being a terrifying, overwhelming force, and becomes a clear, clean source of energy. It becomes the fuel for our boundaries, the fire for our creativity, the fierce, unwavering commitment to our own truth.

The Erinyes are not your enemies. They are the oldest, wisest parts of you, demanding that you live a life of integrity, of sovereignty, of embodied power. Listen to them. Honor them. Let them do their necessary work.

## The Throat Chakra and the Silenced Voice

In the esoteric anatomy of the chakra system, the throat chakra (Vishuddha) is the center of communication, truth, and self-expression. It is the bridge between the heart and the mind. When we swallow our anger, when we bite our tongues to keep the peace, we are energetically strangling this vital center.

The physical manifestations of a blocked throat chakra are profound. We see it in chronic sore throats, in thyroid dysfunction, in tension in the sternocleidomastoid muscles that run down the sides of the neck. We see it in the habitual clearing of the throat, a nervous tic that betrays the body's desperate attempt to dislodge the unspoken words.

The Erinyes demand to be heard. When we deny them a voice, they do not go silent; they simply change their frequency. They become the passive-aggressive comments, the sarcastic remarks, the bitter complaints muttered under the breath. They become the physical constriction that makes it hard to swallow, hard to speak, hard to breathe.

To heal the throat, we must practice the radical act of speaking our truth, even when our voices shake. We must learn to articulate our boundaries clearly, without apology, without justification. We must allow the raw, unpolished sound of our anger to vibrate through our vocal cords. This is not about yelling at others; it is about reclaiming the sonic territory of our own bodies. It is about letting the Furies sing their necessary song.

## The Role of Community in Somatic Healing

We cannot do this work entirely in isolation. The nervous system is a social organ; it regulates itself through connection with others. When we have been shamed for our anger, when we have been told that our Furies make us unlovable, we need the mirroring of a safe community to heal.

We need spaces where our anger is witnessed without judgment, without attempts to fix it or spiritualize it away. We need to see other women standing in their power, expressing their boundaries, and surviving the experience. We need the collective resonance of shared truth-telling.

This is the true meaning of a healing circle. It is not a place to bypass our pain with forced positivity; it is a crucible where the raw material of our human experience can be alchemized. When we bring our anger into the light of a supportive community, it loses its shame. It becomes a shared human experience, a collective force for change. The Erinyes, once isolated and feared, become a chorus of fierce, protective guardians, standing together to defend the sacred boundaries of the community.

## FAQ

**Q: I’m afraid that if I let myself feel my anger, I’ll lose control and hurt someone. What should I do?**
**A:** This is the most common fear, born from a culture that conflates anger with violence. Violence is an action; anger is a physiological state. You can feel the full, terrifying intensity of your anger in your body without ever raising your voice or lifting a hand. The somatic practice is about containing the sensation, not acting it out. When you learn to hold the physical charge of anger in your nervous system, the urge to lash out actually diminishes.

**Q: My jaw is constantly tight, but I don't feel angry. I just feel stressed and overwhelmed. Could it still be anger?**
**A:** Yes. When anger is suppressed early and often, it goes underground. The conscious mind registers "stress" or "anxiety," but the body is holding the muscular tension of a thwarted fight response. The masseter muscles, the psoas, and the trapezius are classic holding sites for unexpressed fury. Start by addressing the physical tension directly, without needing a cognitive story about *why* you are angry. The body's truth will reveal itself in time.

**Q: You mentioned dandelion root for the liver. Can I just take a supplement capsule?**
**A:** You can, but you will miss the medicine of the bitter taste. In herbalism, the taste *is* the action. When the bitter receptors on your tongue are stimulated, they send a vagal nerve signal to your digestive system and liver to begin releasing stagnation. A capsule bypasses this entirely. Brew the strong, bitter tea. Let your body experience the sharpness. It is a somatic practice in itself.

**Q: How do I know the difference between healthy anger and just being stuck in a loop of resentment?**
**A:** Healthy anger is a wave. It rises, peaks, delivers its message ("a boundary has been crossed"), and then, if allowed to complete its somatic cycle, it recedes, leaving you energized and clear. Resentment is stagnant water. It is anger that has not been allowed to move through the body, often because we are replaying the story in our minds rather than feeling the sensation in our tissues. If you are stuck in resentment, drop the story and focus entirely on the physical sensations in your body.

**Q: What if my anger is directed at someone who is no longer in my life, or who has passed away?**
**A:** The body does not know the difference between past and present, or between presence and absence. The physiological charge of the anger is still living in your tissues, regardless of where the other person is. You do not need the other person to complete the somatic cycle. You can process the anger, release the tension, and reclaim your boundaries entirely within your own nervous system. The healing is for you, not for them.

---
